questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The equation of the directrices of the hyperbola $3 x^{2}-3 y^{2}-18 x+12 y+2=0$ is
questions.solution:
Given equation is
$$
3 x^{2}-3 y^{2}-18 x+12 y+2=0
$$
It can be written as
$\frac{(x-3)^{2}}{\left(\sqrt{\frac{13}{3}}\right)^{2}}-\frac{(y-2)^{2}}{\left(\sqrt{\frac{13}{3}}\right)^{2}}=1$
$\begin{aligned} \text { here, } a &=b=\sqrt{\frac{13}{3}} \\ \therefore \quad e &=\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}} \\ &=\sqrt{1+1} \\ &=\sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Equation of the directrix are
$$
x=3 \pm \sqrt{\frac{13}{6}}
$$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If the eccentricity of the hyperbola $x^{2}-y^{2} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \alpha=25$ is $\sqrt{5}$ times the eccentricity of the ellipse $x^{2} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \alpha+y^{2}=5$ then $\alpha$ is equal to :
questions.solution:
Eccentrcity of $\frac{x^{2}}{25}-\frac{y^{2}}{25 \sin ^{2} \alpha}=1$ is $\sqrt{1+\sin ^{2} \alpha}$

Eccentricity of $\frac{x^{2}}{5 \sin ^{2} \alpha}+\frac{y^{2}}{5}=1 \quad$ is $\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \alpha}$
Given, $\sqrt{1+\sin ^{2} \alpha}=\sqrt{5} \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \alpha}$
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow \sin ^{2} \alpha=\frac{2}{3} \\
\Rightarrow \alpha=\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}=\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{2}
\end{array}
$$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A point moves in such a way that the difference of its distance from two points (8,0) and (-8,0) always remains $4 .$ Then, the locus of the point is
questions.solution: We know by the definition of hyperbola, "A hyperbola is the locus of a point which moves in such a way that the difference between two fixed points remains constant, ie., $|P S-P S|=2 a$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The eccentricity of a rectangular hyperbola is
questions.solution:
The general equation of a hyperbola is \(\frac{x^2}{a^2}-\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1\), where \(a\) represents the transverse of hyperbola and \(b\) represents the conjugate of the hyperbola.
The eccentricity of a hyperbola is \(e=\frac{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}{a} \cdots(1)\).
Since a rectangular hyperbola is defined as a hyperbola whose transverse axis is identical to its conjugate axis, \(a=b\).
Substitute \(a=b\) into (1).
\(\begin{aligned}
& e=\frac{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}{a} \\
& \Rightarrow e=\frac{\sqrt{a^2+a^2}}{a} \\
& \Rightarrow e=\frac{\sqrt{2 a^2}}{a} \\
& \Rightarrow e=\frac{\sqrt{2} a}{a} \\
& \Rightarrow e=\sqrt{2}
\end{aligned}\)
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The length of conjugate axis of a hyperbola is greater than the length of transverse axis. Then, the eccentricity e is
questions.solution:
Let the length of conjugate axis $=b$
and the length of transvers axis $=a$ Given that, $b>a$
Now,$b^{2}>a^{2}$
$\therefore$ Eccentricity $(e)=\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}$
$e^{2}=\left(\sqrt{1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}}\right)^{2}>2$
$e>\sqrt{2}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A double ordinate $\mathrm{PQ}$ of the hyperbola $\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}}{\mathrm{a}^{2}}-\frac{\mathrm{y}^{2}}{\mathrm{~b}^{2}}=1$ is such that $\Delta \mathrm{OPQ}$ is equilateral, $\mathrm{O}$ being the centre of the hyperbola. Then the eccentricity e satisfies the relation
questions.solution: Hint: $P \equiv(a \sec \theta, b \tan \theta) \angle \mathrm{POM}=\tan 30^{\circ}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}} \sin \theta$ $\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{a}{b \sqrt{3}}$ $\Rightarrow \frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}>\frac{1}{3} \quad \Rightarrow 1+\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}>\frac{4}{3} \quad \Rightarrow e>\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The distance between the foci of the hyperbola $x^2-3 y^2-4 x-6 y-11=0$ is
questions.solution:
Given, equation of hyperbola is
$$
\begin{gathered}
x^2-3 y^2-4 x-6 y-11=0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad\left(x^2-4 x+4\right)-3\left(y^2+2 y+1\right)-11 \\
=4-3 \\
\Rightarrow \quad(x-2)^2-3(y+1)^2=12 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{(x-2)^2}{12}-\frac{(y+1)^2}{4}=1 \\
\text { Now, } \quad e=\sqrt{1+\frac{4}{12}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}
\end{gathered}
$$
Now,
$$
e=\sqrt{1+\frac{4}{12}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}
$$
$\therefore$ Distance between foci
$$
=2 a e=2 \times \sqrt{12} \times \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}=8
$$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A hyperbola passing through a focus of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{169}+\frac{y^2}{25}=1$. Its transverse and conjugate axes coincide respectively with the major and minor axes of the ellipse. The product of eccentricities is 1 . Then, the equation of the hyperbola is
questions.solution:
Let the equation of hyperbola be
$$
\frac{x^2}{a^2}-\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1
$$
Given equation of ellipse is
$$
\frac{x^2}{(13)^2}+\frac{y^2}{(5)^2}=1
$$
Here,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { Here, } \quad a=13, b=5 \\
& \therefore \quad e=\sqrt{1-\frac{b^2}{a^2}} \\
& \therefore \quad=\sqrt{1-\frac{25}{169}}=\sqrt{\frac{144}{169}}=\frac{12}{13} \\
& \therefore \text { Focus }( \pm a e, 0)=\left( \pm 13 \times \frac{12}{13}, 0\right) \\
& \quad=( \pm 12,0)
\end{aligned}
$$
Since, Eq. (i) passes through $( \pm 12,0)$.
$$
\begin{array}{rrrl}
\therefore & \frac{144}{a^2}-\frac{0}{b^2}=1 \\
\Rightarrow & a^2=144 \\
\Rightarrow & a= \pm 12
\end{array}
$$
Now eccentricity of hyperbola
$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{\prime} & =\sqrt{1+\frac{b^2}{a^2}} \\
& =\sqrt{1+\frac{b^2}{144}}
\end{aligned}
$$
According to the equation,
$$
\begin{array}{rlrl} 
& e^{\prime}=1 \\
\Rightarrow & & \frac{12}{13} \times \sqrt{1+\frac{b^2}{144}} & =1 \\
\Rightarrow & & \sqrt{1+\frac{b^2}{144}} & =\frac{13}{12} \\
\Rightarrow & & 1+\frac{b^2}{144} & =\frac{169}{144} \\
\Rightarrow & & \frac{b^2}{144} & =\frac{169}{144}-1 \\
\Rightarrow & & \frac{b^2}{144} & =\frac{25}{144} \\
b^2 & =25
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore$ Equation of hyperbola is
$$
\frac{x^2}{144}-\frac{y^2}{25}=1
$$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The distance between the foci of the hyperbola $x^2-3 y^2-4 x-6 y-11=0$ is
questions.solution:
Given, equation of hyperbola is
$$
\begin{gathered}
x^2-3 y^2-4 x-6 y-11=0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad\left(x^2-4 x+4\right)-3\left(y^2+2 y+1\right)-11 \\
=4-3 \\
\Rightarrow \quad(x-2)^2-3(y+1)^2=12 \\
\Rightarrow \quad \frac{(x-2)^2}{12}-\frac{(y+1)^2}{4}=1 \\
\text { Now, } \quad e=\sqrt{1+\frac{4}{12}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}
\end{gathered}
$$
Now,
$$
e=\sqrt{1+\frac{4}{12}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}
$$
$\therefore$ Distance between foci
$$
=2 a e=2 \times \sqrt{12} \times \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}=8
$$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If the focii of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{25}+\frac{y^2}{16}=1$ and the hyperbola $\frac{x^2}{4}-\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ coincide, then $b^2$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given, equation of ellipse is
$$
\frac{x^2}{25}+\frac{y^2}{16}=1
$$
and equation of hyperbola is
$$
\frac{x^2}{4}-\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1
$$
eccentricity of ellipse
$$
\begin{aligned}
& b^2=a^2\left(1-e^2\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 16=25\left(1-e^2\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \quad e^2=1-\frac{16}{25}=\frac{9}{25} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad e= \pm \frac{3}{5} \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$
Focii of the ellipse $=( \pm a e, 0)=( \pm 3,0)$ which coincide with focii of the hyperbola.
Let $e_1$ be the eccentricity of the hyperbola.
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\therefore & \pm a e_1= \pm 3 \\
\Rightarrow & e_1=\frac{3}{2}
\end{array}
$$
Now, $\quad b^2=a^2\left(e_1^2-1\right)$
$$
\begin{array}{cc}
\Rightarrow & b^2=4\left(\frac{9}{4}-1\right)=4 \times \frac{5}{4} \\
\Rightarrow & b^2=5
\end{array}
$$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The equation <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><msqrt><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></msqrt><mo>-</mo><msqrt><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></msqrt></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> will represent a hyperbola for -
questions.solution:
We have x2+y-12-x2+y+12= K

Which is equivalent to S1P-S2P=Const.

Where S1≡ 0, 1, S2≡ 0, -1  .and P≡ x, y

Using properties of a hyperbola, the above equation represents a hyperbola, then we have.

2a=K [where 2a is the transverse axis and e is the eccentricity]

and 2ae=S1 S2=2

Dividing, we have e =2K

Since, e > 1 for a hyperbola, therefore K < 2

Also, K must be a positive quantity. Hence, we have, K∈ 0, 2 .
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: For the hyperbola $x^2-y^2-4 x+2 y+c=0$, if the focus is $S(2+2 \sqrt{2}, k)$ and the directrix that is adjacent to $S$ is $x=2+\sqrt{2}$, then $c=$
questions.solution:
Given hyperbola
$\begin{aligned}
x^2-y^2-4 x+2 y+c & =0 \\
(x-2)^2-(y-1)^2 & =3-c \\
\frac{(x-2)^2}{3-c}-\frac{(y-1)^2}{3-c} & =1
\end{aligned}$
Focus of hyperbola is
$\begin{aligned}
x-2 & = \pm \sqrt{(3-c)+(3-c)} \\
x-2 & = \pm \sqrt{6-2 c} \quad[\because a e=\sqrt{(3-c)+(3-c)}] \\
x & =2 \pm \sqrt{6-2 c}
\end{aligned}$
Here, $2+\sqrt{6-2 c}=2+2 \sqrt{2}$
$\therefore \quad \sqrt{6-2 c}=2 \sqrt{2} \Rightarrow 6-2 c=8 \Rightarrow c=-1$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If $e_1$ is the eccentricity of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{25}=1$ and $e_2$ is the eccentricity of a hyperbola passing through the foci of the given ellipse and $e_1 e_2=1$, then the equation of such a hyperbola among the following is
questions.solution:
Equation of ellipse $\frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{25}=1$
Foci $\quad=(0, \pm 3)$
$\Rightarrow \quad e_2=\sqrt{1-\frac{16}{25}}=\frac{3}{5}$ given $e_2 e_2=1$
(where $e_2$ is eccentricity of hyperbola)
Let equation of hyperbola $-\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$
its passes through $(0, \pm 3)$
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\therefore & b^2=9 \\
\Rightarrow & e_2=\sqrt{1+\frac{a^2}{b^2}} \\
\Rightarrow & e_2^2=1+\frac{a^2}{b^2} \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{1}{e_2^2}=1+\frac{a^2}{9} \Rightarrow \frac{25}{9}=1+\frac{a^2}{9} \\
\Rightarrow & a^2=16
\end{array}
$$
Hence, the equation of hyperbola is $\frac{y^2}{9}-\frac{x^2}{16}=1$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: The equation of the hyperbola having eccentricity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>, such that the distance between its foci is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> and the major axis is along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis, while the minor axis is along the<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></math>-axis, is
questions.solution:
Distance between foci =8=2ae

But e=2. Therefore, 2a=4

⇒a=2⇒a2=4

And b2=a2e2-1=44-1=12

∴Equation of hyperbola is x24-y212=1
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: A hyperbola having its centre at the origin is passing through the point $(5,2)$ and has transverse axis of length 8 along the $\mathrm{X}$-axis. Then the eccentricity of its conjugate hyperbola is
questions.solution:
Let equation of hyperbola is $\frac{x^2}{a^2}-\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$
Given, $2 \mathrm{a}=8 \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=4$
It passes through $(5,2)$
Hence, on solving we get, $\mathrm{b}=\frac{8}{3}$
Now, eccentricity of its conjugate hyperbola is
$$
=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2}{\mathrm{~b}^2}}=\frac{\sqrt{13}}{2}
$$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Let $P\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right), Q\left(\frac{5 \pi}{4}\right), R\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right), T\left(\frac{7 \pi}{4}\right)$ be the points on the hyperbola $x^2-4 y^2-4=0$ in the parametric form. Then the area of the quadrilateral $P Q R T$ is (in square units)
questions.solution:
Equation of hyperbola $x^2-4 y^2-4=0$
$$
\frac{x^2}{4}-\frac{y^2}{1}=1
$$
Then, $a=2, b=1$
Parametric point on hyperbola $=(a \sec \theta, b \tan \theta)$
Then, $\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\left(2 \sec \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right), 1 \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right)$
$$
=(2 \sqrt{2}, 1)
$$
Similarly, Q $\left(\frac{5 \pi}{4}\right)=\left(2 \sec \left(\frac{5 \pi}{4}\right), 1 \tan \left(\frac{5 \pi}{4}\right)\right)$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =(-2 \sqrt{2}, 1) \\
& \mathrm{R}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)=(-2 \sqrt{2},-1) \\
& \mathrm{T}\left(\frac{7 \pi}{4}\right)=(2 \sqrt{2},-1)
\end{aligned}
$$
Area of quadrilateral PQRT(sq. unit)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{1}{2}\left\{\left(x_1-x_3\right)\left(y_2-y_4\right)-\left(x_2-x_4\right)\left(y_1-y_3\right)\right\} \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\{(2 \sqrt{2}+2 \sqrt{2})(1+1)-(-2 \sqrt{2}-2 \sqrt{2})(1+1)\} \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\{4 \sqrt{2} \times 2+4 \sqrt{2} \times 2\} \\
& =\frac{1}{2} \times 16 \sqrt{2}=8 \sqrt{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
So, option (d) is correct.
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If two points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> lie on the hyperbola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mo>(</mo><mi>a</mi><mo><</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>)</mo></math>, whose centre <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> be such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mi>P</mi></math> is perpendicular to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mi>Q</mi></math>, then the value of <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>C</mi><msup><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>C</mi><msup><mrow><mi>Q</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac></math> is
questions.solution:
Let CP=r1 be inclined to transverse axis at an angle θ such that Pr1cos⁡θ, r1sin⁡θ lies on the hyperbola. Therefore,

r12 cos2⁡θa2-sin2⁡θb2=1

Replacing θ by 90o+θ, we get, 

r22 sin2⁡θa2-cos2⁡θb2=1

(where, CQ=r2)

⇒  1r12+1r22=cos2⁡θa2 -sin2⁡θb2+sin2⁡θa2-cos2⁡θb2

⇒  1r12+1r22=cos2⁡θ1a2-1b2+sin2⁡θ1a2-1b2

⇒  1r12+1r22=1a2-1b2

⇒  1CP2+1CQ2=1a2-1b2
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: If a directrix of a hyperbola centered at the origin and passing through the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math> and its eccentricity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math>, then:
questions.solution:
Let equation of hyperbola is x2a2-y2b2=1

∴ It passes through 4, -23  ⇒16a2-12b2=1

⇒16-12×a2b2=a2...1

Equation of directrix is x=ae=45, given in question.

⇒a2=165e2...2

And we know that b2=a2e2-1

⇒b2a2=e2-1...3

∴ From 1,2 & 3

16-12e2-1=165e2

⇒16e2-16-12=16e25e2-1

⇒ 4e4-24e2+35=0
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Let $A$ be a square matrix of order 2 such that $|A|=2$ and the sum of its diagonal elements is -3 . If the points $(x, y)$ satisfying $\mathrm{A}^2+x \mathrm{~A}+y \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{O}$ lie on a hyperbola, whose $\text { length of semi major axis is } x \text { and semi minor axis is } y$, eccentricity is $\mathrm{e}$ and the length of the latus rectum is $l$, then $81\left(e^ 4+l^2\right)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given $|A|=2$
trace $A=-3$
and $\mathrm{A}^2+\mathrm{xA}+\mathrm{yI}=0$
 $\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=3, \mathrm{y}=2$ 
$\begin{aligned}
& \text {so, } 81 e^{4}=169 \\
& L R=8 / 9 \\
& 81 e^{4}+81 L^{2}=169+64=233 \end{aligned}$
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
questions.q: Let  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><msup><mi>L</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></math> be the latus rectum through the focus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> of a hyperbola and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> be the farther vertex of the conic. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>Δ</mo><mo> </mo><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mi>L</mi><msup><mi>L</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></math> is equilateral then its eccentricity is
questions.solution:
AM=a+ae

ML'=b2a

θ=30°

⇒  b2aa+ae=13

⇒b2a2=e+13

⇒  e2-1=e+13

⇒ e-1=13

⇒ e=1+13
questions.source: Highly selective Backlog Qs for JEE Main
